title: Start Simple, Grow Complex

content.statement: Every working complex system grew out of a working simple one.
content.explainer: You cannot design a complex system that works from scratch; complexity that works is always evolved, never installed. So launch something small that works and let it earn its complexity.
questions: Are you trying to launch a fully complex system at once, or a simple one that actually works today?
Which parts of your grand design are unproven complexity you're adding before the core even functions?
Can you point to the working simple version that your current complex plan is supposed to have grown from?
sop: Write down the smallest version of your system that would genuinely work end to end for one real user.
Rate how simple that launch version is on a 1-10 scale (see calculation, page 6).
Rate how much complexity you're actually planning to ship at launch on a 1-10 scale (see calculation, page 6).
Divide simplicity by complexity; if the ratio is below 1, strip features until the working core stands alone.
Ship the simple working version first, then add complexity only where real usage proves it's needed.

definitions.def: A minimal version that genuinely functions end to end, not a stripped-down mockup. Only this can reliably evolve into a working complex system.
Complexity that was added gradually onto a functioning base, each layer tested by real use, as opposed to complexity designed in all at once.

content.equation: Survival Odds = Simplicity at Launch ÷ Complexity at Launch
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Simplicity at launch 8, complexity at launch 2. Survival Odds = 8 ÷ 2 = 4, comfortably above 1, meaning the system is simple enough to actually work and then grow. A ratio below 1 (complex and tangled at launch) signals a system likely to collapse before it ever stabilizes.
content.closing_statement: Build things that work small first, and let them earn every layer of complexity they carry.
content.question: Are you trying to launch a fully complex system at once, or a simple one that actually works today?

caption:
Principle Nº 334: Start Simple, Grow Complex

Every working complex system grew out of a working simple one.

Ask yourself: Are you trying to launch a fully complex system at once, or a simple one that actually works today?
